coredata.dc:description: The achievement of sensitive and intuitive sensing residual antibiotics is of paramount significance to food safety and human health. Herein, we developed a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nano-sensor based on bipyridine-functionalized fluorine, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (F, N-CDs@bipy) to cascade sense copper ion (Cu2 +) and ciprofloxacin (CIP). During the detection process, Cu2+ not only serves as analyte, but also acts as a bridge between CIP and F, N-CDs@bipy. The mutual factors of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and static complex formation induce fluorescence quenching, while the emulative coordination effect leads to the recovery of F, N-CDs@bipy dual-emission. Prepared nano-sensor demonstrates a direct proportionality to Cu2+ (0.056–30 μM) with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 56 nM. Subsequently, the introduction of CIP triggers the chelation between Cu2+ and CIP to release the fluorescence (linear response: 0.059–20 µM and LOD=59 nM). Furthermore, a portable smartphone platform is designed to visual monitor Cu2+ and CIP in real samples, achieving LODs of 112 nM and 126 nM, respectively. Therefore, our work not only provides a reliable way for visual detection of Cu2+ and CIP based on F, N-CDs@bipy, but also offers a promising strategy for constructing food safety detection.

head.ce:title: Bipyridine-functionalized F, N-doped carbon dots featuring dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence for cascade visual sensing of copper ion and ciprofloxacin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The achievement of sensitive and intuitive sensing residual antibiotics is of paramount significance to food safety and human health. Herein, we developed a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nano-sensor based on bipyridine-functionalized fluorine, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (F, N-CDs@bipy) to cascade sense copper ion (Cu2 +) and ciprofloxacin (CIP). During the detection process, Cu2+ not only serves as analyte, but also acts as a bridge between CIP and F, N-CDs@bipy. The mutual factors of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and static complex formation induce fluorescence quenching, while the emulative coordination effect leads to the recovery of F, N-CDs@bipy dual-emission. Prepared nano-sensor demonstrates a direct proportionality to Cu2+ (0.056–30 μM) with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 56 nM. Subsequently, the introduction of CIP triggers the chelation between Cu2+ and CIP to release the fluorescence (linear response: 0.059–20 µM and LOD=59 nM). Furthermore, a portable smartphone platform is designed to visual monitor Cu2+ and CIP in real samples, achieving LODs of 112 nM and 126 nM, respectively. Therefore, our work not only provides a reliable way for visual detection of Cu2+ and CIP based on F, N-CDs@bipy, but also offers a promising strategy for constructing food safety detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a vital microelement, copper ion (Cu2+) is essential for human health and plays a key role in numerous physiological functions, including iron absorption, hemopoiesis, blood formation, connective tissue development and various enzyme activities (Echabaane et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2021). The average daily intake of Cu2+ for an adult is approximately 1.2 mg to maintain health (Ávila et al., 2022). However, excessive Cu2+ ingestion or prolonged exposure to high levels of Cu2+ can be harmful, as Cu2+ is less degradable and prone to bioaccumulation. This can increase the production of reactive oxygen species, leading to damage to the liver, kidneys and central nervous system, potentially causing serious diseases such as Alzheimer’s, Wilson’s and Menkes diseases (Echabaane et al., 2021; Moshier and Schwarberg, 1966). Furthermore, Cu2+, being a heavy metal, has widespread industrial applications, contributing to severe environmental pollution (Hu et al., 2021). The United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) has set a standard for the allowed concentration of Cu2+ in industrial wastewater, which should be below 1.3 mg/L (Lu et al., 2020a). Given these considerations, developing a rapid, accurate and reliable method for detecting Cu2+ in biological and ambient water samples is crucial.

Ciprofloxacin (CIP), a fluoroquinolone antibiotic with broad-spectrum activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, is widely used in medical and agricultural sectors to combat infections (Wolf et al., 2019; Krzek et al., 2005). Its ability to form stable complexes with metal ions, particularly Cu2+, is attributed to its multiple coordination functional groups (Khurana et al., 2021). However, the overuse of antibiotics like CIP has led to the emergence of carbapenem resistance and reduced the efficacy of antibiotic treatments, posing a significant threat to global public health (Rezende et al., 2019). Additionally, excessive CIP intake has been linked to adverse effects such as gastrointestinal dysbiosis, central nervous system lesions, skin photosensitivity and eosinophilia, underscoring the need for monitoring CIP residues in the environment and food products (Lu et al., 2020b). Hence, it is momentous to indemnify global public security measured by the residue determination of CIP.

Traditional detection methods for Cu2+ and CIP, such as electrochemical methods, gas chromatography (GC), fiber-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (FERS) and high-performance thin-layer chromatography (HPTLC), require complex sample preparation, expensive equipment and skilled operators, limiting their application in rapid and precise analysis (Echabaane et al., 2021; Moshier and Schwarberg, 1966; Wolf et al., 2019; Krzek et al., 2005). In contrast, fluorescence spectroscopy offers a sensitive, selective and operationally simple alternative for the analysis of Cu2+ and CIP. Carbon dots (CDs), in particular, have emerged as promising fluorescent nanomaterials for sensing due to their low cytotoxicity, green synthesis, excellent biocompatibility and unique optical properties. For instance, Man et al. developed blue triazine-doped CDs for Cu2+ detection (Man et al., 2021), while Lu et al. created a label-free platform using luminous yellow carbon dots for the detection of fluoroquinolones (Lu et al., 2018). However, these assays are often limited by single-emission detection, which can be influenced by factors such as material concentration, equipment performance and environmental conditions (Hu et al., 2024). To overcome these challenges, the development of ratiometric fluorescence CDs is highly advantageous, as it allows for the calibration of systematic errors by comparing the emission intensities of target and reference signals, thereby expanding the detection range and enhancing accuracy (Wang et al., 2019). Furthermore, with the increasing demand for sensitive and precise real-time monitoring, smartphone-based detection platforms have gained attention for their ease of use and portability, offering a platform to improve the accessibility and reliability of sensors (Qian et al., 2020). Therefore, it is sorely urgent to build a feasible and simple route to detect Cu2+ and CIP using ratiometric fluorescent probes with dual emission and employ smartphone devices for on-site, rapid and visual detection.

4, 4’-bipyridine (bipyridine) provides the possibility to construct fluorophore-grafted CDs and metal detection probes because of its avid metal coordination capacity and characteristic photophysical properties (Jia et al., 2021; Fu et al., 2017). Motivated by the above consideration, an innovative ratiometric fluorescence-based nano-sensor is engineered for cascade sensing Cu2+ and CIP ( Scheme 1). Norfloxacin (Noroxin) and bipyridine undergo hydrothermal process to successfully synthesize bipyridine-functionalized fluorine, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (F, N-CDs@bipy), which exhibit bright dual-emission fluorescence at 440 nm and 510 nm. Upon the introduction of Cu2+, a notable decrease of the system fluorescence intensity is observed via the generation of a static quenching compound coupled with a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process from the F, N-CDs@bipy to Cu2+. Developed sensor confirms a robust linear regression line of Cu2+ concentration (0.056–30 μM) and the excellent limit of detection (LOD, 56 nM). Furthermore, CIP, as a potent chelator of Cu2+, can competitively coordinate with Cu2+, thereby sequestering them from the F, N-CDs@bipy and consequently restoring the fluorescence intensity. Post-introduction of 200 μM Cu2+ into the F, N-CDs@bipy system, the nano-sensor demonstrates heightened sensitivity for CIP detection within 0.059–20 μM and a LOD of 59 nM. Furthermore, a detection platform integrated smartphone is developed to facilitate direct and instantaneous optical analysis of Cu2+ and CIP within genuine samples, which is characterized by the brilliant LODs of 112 nM for Cu2+ and 126 nM for CIP. Therefore, this research designs a straightforward, durable and recyclable ratio-fluorescence nano-sensor for the selective quantification of Cu2+ and CIP, further providing a kind of smartphone-assisted tote device applied in the immediate and in-situ assessment of practical samples.

Noroxin (98.00 %), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.9 %), bipyridine (98.00 %), CIP (≥98.0 %), CuCl2, NaOH, H2SO4, AgCl, CoCl2, MgSO4, CdCl2, PbCl2, CaCl2, CeCl3, FeCl3, FeCl2, BaCl2, AlCl3, HgCl2, ZnCl2, CrCl3, NaCl, K2CO3, resorcinol (Res, 99.00 %), catechol (Cat, ≥99.5 %), hydroquinone (HDQ, 99.00 %), proline (Pro, 99.5 %), glutathione (Glu, 98.00 %), tryptophan (Trp, 99.00 %), leucine (Leu, 99.5 %) L-Cysteine (L-Cys, ≥99.5 %) L-Alanine (L-Ala, 99.00 %), L-Serine (L-Ser, 98.00 %), L-Aspartic acid (L-Asp, ≥98.0 %), pefloxacin (PEF, ≥99.5 %), fleroxacin (FLE, 98.00 %), levofloxacin (Lev, ≥98.0 %), tetracycline (TC, ≥99.5 %), oxytetracycline (OTC, 99.5 %), chlortetracycline (CTC, 99.00 %), doxycycline (DOX, ≥98.0 %) and addition chemicals were procured from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All solvents and reagents were of the analytical grade form and were employed without the implementation of any additional purification procedures. Ultra-pure deionized water with a resistance of 18.2 MΩ, generated via a Millipore water purification system (Milli-Q, Millipore), was employed in all experimental procedures.

The size and dimensional attribute of F, N-CDs@bipy were ascertained through the application of a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Model JEM-2100F, Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Ltd.). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was conducted utilizing an X-Ray Diffractometer (Empyrean, Malvern Panalytical, Netherlands). The Raman spectral profiling was conducted using a Thermo Fisher Scientific DXi Raman spectrometer, illuminated by an Ar+ laser emitting at a wavelength of 532 nm for the purpose of sample excitation. The morphology of F, N-CDs@bipy is characterized by an atomic force microscope (AFM, Oxford Instruments, Cypher S). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and the ensemble of photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were respectively documented using a UV-5800 spectrophotometer (Metash, China) and a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, UK). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by a Vertex 70 FT-IR spectroscope (Bruker Corp, Germany). Concurrently, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on the Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer (Kratos Analytical Ltd., UK). Transient photoluminescence spectra were recorded on a FLS 980-STM spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK) at ambient temperature. iPhone 13 smartphone was used to record fluorescence images under the illumination of an AGL-9406 UV lamp.

1 mmol noroxin and 20 mL DMF were mixed thoroughly with ultrasonic treatment, followed by the conventional hydrothermal process (200 °C, 4 h) to synthesize F, N-doped carbon dots (F, N-CDs). Next, upon natural cooling of the high-pressure reaction kettle to ambient temperature, the resulting solid and liquid phases were effectively partitioned through vacuum-assisted filtration. Dispersed F, N-CDs from the liquid phase underwent subsequent purification through a dialysis process and were ultimately retrieved through lyophilization. After that, 1 mmol bipyridine was mixed with purified F, N-CDs solution, following the mixture was further reheated at 200 °C for 2 h and free cooled to obtain tentative F, N-CDs@bipy solution. The untreated solution was purified through 0.22 μm microporous membrane and then dialyzed across 48 h using a dialysis tubing with 500-Da molecular weight to eliminate impurities. Finally, pristine F, N-CDs@bipy powder was produced via freeze drying the supernatant for subsequent sensing.

Rhodamine 6 G (R6G), possessing quantum yield (QY) of 95 % in ethanol excited by 420 nm, was selected as the benchmark. To attenuate the influence of self-absorption, the absorbance of R6G and F, N-CDs@bipy were regulated to remain beneath the threshold of 0.1. Subsequently, the calculation of fluorescence QY was conducted through a standardized formula (Dang et al., 2020):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing for Cu2+ and CIP
ce:section.ce:para: The working solution was prepared by dissolving the as-prepared F, N-CDs@bipy powder in 20 mL of buffer solution (pH= 7). The pH level of buffers was adjusted by the NaOH (1 M) or HCl (1 M) solutions. Then, 1.5 mL of the obtained F, N-CDs@bipy solution (2 mg‧mL−1) and incremental concentrations of Cu2+ solution (0–400 μM; 100 μL) were added to a quartz cuvette, followed by diluting the mixture to 3.0 mL with pH= 7 buffer solution. After uniform-mixing and incubating for 3 min at room temperature (25 ± 0.5 °C), emission spectra were recorded by 340 nm excitation. Subsequently, CIP was detected by ratiometric fluorescence enhancement. A series of CIP standard solutions (0–100 μM, 100 μL) were added to the F, N-CDs@bipy-200 μM Cu2+ (the volume ratio of F, N-CDs@bipy and Cu2+ is 2:1). Following a 3-minute cultivation period, two fluorescence peaks (440 nm and 510 nm) were obtained and corresponding intensities were recorded.

Additionally, fluorescence of the F, N-CDs@bipy was monitored with the addition of 34 different interfering chemicals to test the selectivity of the sensor. Specifically, the fluorescence intensity of F, N-CDs@bipy without Cu2+ was recorded under 100 μM other kinds of metal ions (Ag+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Na+, K+) and 600 μM molecules including Res, Cat, HDQ, Pro, Glu, Trp, Leu, L-Cys, L-Ala, L-Ser, L-Asp, PEF, FLE, Lev, TC, OTC, CTC and DOX. Meanwhile, a series of same interfering ions and substances (100 μM and 600 μM) were added into F, N-CDs@bipy solution with 100 μM Cu2+ and the fluorescence intensity was recorded under 340 nm excitation wavelength.

Real samples (tap water and meat) were procured from a local market adjacent to Ningbo University. A 100 mL aliquot of the tap water sample was passed through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter to eliminate solid impurities. Next, the treated liquid sample was fortified with varying concentrations of Cu2+ and CIP (with an addition of 200 μM Cu2+) and then thoroughly mixed for subsequent analysis. For the meat sample, 5 g was precisely measured into a 50 mL centrifuge tube, followed by the addition of appropriate concentrations of Cu2+ and CIP (with an addition of 200 μM Cu2+). Subsequently, adding 10 mL of water to the tube and the contents were thoroughly mixed. Ultimately, after centrifuging the sample for 10 minutes at 5000 rpm, 5 mL of the supernatant was carefully transferred for follow-up detection (Xing et al., 2020).

Considering that independent dual-emission centers (λem=440 nm and 510 nm) of F, N-CDs@bipy will be affected by the synthesis conditions, impacts of various synthesis parameters including hydrothermal temperature, hydrothermal duration and raw material ratio are optimized. The variation trend of fluorescence intensity of dual-emission centers in F, N-CDs@bipy is explored under different hydrothermal temperature ( Fig. 1 (a)). The fluorescence intensity gradually increases as the temperature rises from 100 to 200 °C and then it continuously decreases as the temperature further increases to 250 °C. Moreover, the influence of hydrothermal duration is shown in Fig. 1 (b), in which the dual-emission fluorescence attains its apex at a hydrothermal treatment time of 6 hours. Fig. 1 (c) indicates that the raw material ratio (noroxin:bipyridine) of 1:1 is optimal. Therefore, the hydrothermal temperature of 200 °C, hydrothermal duration of 6 hours and the raw material ratio of 1:1 are selected as the optimal synthesis conditions to F, N-CDs@bipy.

The morphologies of F, N-CDs@bipy are revealed through TEM image, which indicates the widely-dispersed symmetrical distribution of developed F, N-CDs@bipy (Fig. 1 (d)). Aligned with the (100) plane of graphite, the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) inset displays a lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm for F, N-CDs@bipy, which uncovers the presence of graphite layer structure in the material. The average size of F, N-CDs@bipy is determined as 4.0 nm (Fig. 1 (e)). Meanwhile, Raman spectroscopy and the XRD method are used to investigate lattice properties of F, N-CDs@bipy. Figure S1 delineates the presence of a sp3-hybridized carbon bond (D peak) at 1321 cm⁻¹ and a sp2-hybridized carbon bond (G peak) at 1581 cm⁻¹ . The measured intensity ratio of D peak to G peak (I D/I G) is 1.08, which is indicative of the high crystallinity within the F, N-CDs@bipy (Wang et al., 2021a). As depicted in Fig. 1 (f), XRD pattern reveals a pronounced broad diffraction peak at approximately 23° correspond to a 0.26 nm interplanar spacing (Wang et al., 2022). In addition, the AFM is employed to ascertain the morphological height of the nanomaterial. Fig. 1 (g) displays the uniform dispersion of F, N-CDs@bipy, while the thickness measurements along two dotted lines in Fig. 1 (g) are statistically analyzed in Fig. 1 (h-i). The calculated average height of 0.85 nm intimates the existence of a limited number of stacked graphite layers within the F, N-CDs@bipy (Fig. 1 (j)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical characteristics and emission mechanism of F, N-CDs@bipy
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, the UV–vis absorption, PL and PLE spectra of F, N-CDs and F, N-CDs@bipy are applied in characterizing optical properties. As depicted in Fig. 2 (a), the UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibits a series of distinct absorption bands, indicative of complex electronic structure to F, N-CDs. Concretely, intrinsic transition (π-π*) attributed to sp2 carbon core (CC) is translated into dual absorption bands at 225 nm and 265 nm, while n-π* transition originated from heteroatomic bond (CN/CO) is represented by the signal around 326 nm (Xu et al., 2024). The PL spectrum of F, N-CDs delineates a maximum peak situated in 440 nm, corresponding to the optimal excitation condition of 340 nm. In addition, the F, N-CDs dispersion with an excitation-independent attribute, as portrayed in Fig. 2 (b-c), indicating a homogeneous and robust fluorescence emission center on the surface. By contrast, in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of F, N-CDs@bipy, a characteristic peak centered at 410 nm is attributed to the intrinsic absorption profile of the bipyridine moiety and the F, N-CDs@bipy maintain intrinsic peak positions to the pristine F, N-CDs (Fig. 2 (d)). Moreover, the emission spectrum of F, N-CDs@bipy presents dual emissions at 440 nm (Center 1) and 510 nm (Center 2) when irradiated with 340 nm, but only Center 2 can be acquired upon excited at 410 nm. As shown in Fig. 2 (e), Center 2 of F, N-CDs@bipy is independent of excitation associated with an exclusive molecular fluorophore. The two-dimensional (2D) fluorescence matrix graph map of F, N-CDs@bipy embodies separate dual-emission centers in Fig. 2 (f). Notably, by comparing the 2D fluorescence matrix graph maps before and after grafting bipyridine, it further proves that the emission center of 440 nm is attributed to F, N-CDs, while the other emission center at 510 nm is related to the grafting of bipyridine. Furthermore, in light of the pronounced spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of F, N-CDs and the excitation spectrum of the bipyridine, it is observed that blue emission of F, N-CDs@bipy at 440 nm is significantly reduced and a strong green emission appears excited from 300 to 470 nm. This phenomenon is vest in the existence of energy transfer from F, N-CDs to the grafted bipyridine, which will enhance the fluorescence intensity of the compact green emission region (Park et al., 2019).

Combined with the luminescence characteristics before and after grafting bipyridine, intrinsic formation mechanism of F, N-CDs@bipy is studied. Initially, noroxin, as a precursor, is mixed with DMF solution and reacted at 200 °C to decompose into fractional carbon fragments. Next, carbon fragments undergo a series of cycloaddition, polymerization and crosslinking to form eventual F, N-CDs. Moreover, fluoride ions, known for their high electronegativity, frequently act as nucleophiles and are also common as leaving groups in the realm of organic substitution reactions, notably within aromatic hydrocarbon chemistry (Tang et al., 2022a). As an aromatic halogenated compound, F, N-CDs can engage in the nucleophilic substitution reaction on the C-F bond with bipyridine. The nucleophilic attack on F, N-CDs results in the creation of defect sites, which facilitate the generation of grafting groups (Vlasov, 1993). Therefore, C-F bond loosens and original fluorine atom is gradually displaced by bipyridine during the hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 2 h, finally leading to the formation of F, N-CDs@bipy. Distinct from the single emission of F, N-CDs, bimodal emission performance of F, N-CDs@bipy occurs and the new green emission is either a free fluorescent molecule or a grafted molecular fluorophore. To clarify the mechanism, we conduct dialysis on F, N-CDs@bipy solution and contrast the 2D fluorescence matrix graph maps under different dialysis time. Dialyzed F, N-CDs@bipy samples yield consistent patterns across all time, suggesting that the fluorescence of Center 2 comes from the successful grafting of bipyridine rather than the independent emission (Figure S2) (Swain et al., 2022).

In addition, the effects of different functional groups on F, N-CDs@bipy emission are elucidated by the FT-IR and XPS spectra. As shown in Figure S3 (a), towards bipyridine and noroxin, various stretching vibration peaks at 3456 cm−1 (O-H/N-H), 2953 cm−1 (C-H), 1462 cm−1 (CN) and 1355 cm−1 (C-N) can be clearly observed, which are inherited by the FT-IR spectrum of F, N-CDs@bipy (Tang et al., 2022b). Meanwhile, both the FT-IR spectra of F, N-CDs and F, N-CDs@bipy exhibit characteristic absorption peaks of 3456 cm−1 (O-H/N-H), 2953 cm−1 (C-H), 1724 cm−1 (C-O) and 1462 cm−1 (CO) (Figure S3 (b)) (Hua et al., 2018). Specifically, the signals at 1260 cm−1 and 1101 cm−1 for F, N-CDs are indicative of the semi-ionic C-F and covalent C-F deformation vibration, respectively (Sim et al., 2020). However, critical covalent C-F disappears for F, N-CDs@bipy, which further supports the grafting of -bipy groups on F, N-CDs surface via the nucleophilic substitution reaction. Additional XPS characterizations are carried out to quantify the difference in elemental and functional group contents before and after grafting with the bipyridine. Four main peaks corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and F 1 s are located in 285.49 eV, 399.79 eV, 531.79 eV and 693.93 eV in the XPS spectra of the F, N-CDs and F, N-CDs@bipy (Figure S4). Detailed atomic concentrations are shown in Figure S5. The obvious increase in the weight ratio of C and N content suggests the successful attachment of bipyridine on the F, N-CDs. More importantly, the decrease of F content proves the occurrence of nucleophilic substitution reaction. In the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra of F, N-CDs and F, N-CDs@bipy, four peaks (C-C/CC, C-N/C-O, C-F and CO) are deconvoluted at 284.30 eV, 285.78 eV, 287.92 eV and 291.64 eV, in which the C-F bond of F, N-CDs@bipy decreases significantly (Fig. 2 (g)) (Yu et al., 2020). This change is due to the nucleophilic substitution reaction between C-F bond of F, N-CDs and pyridine nitrogen in bipyridine, which consumes C-F bonds on F, N-CDs and introduces bipyridine grafting. Meanwhile, more complete graphitization degree and expanded sp2 aromatic scopes in F, N-CDs@bipy are proved by the elevated C-C/CC content (Table S1) (Kalita et al., 2012). Quadruple distinct nitrogen-containing function groups in F, N-CDs and F, N-CDs@bipy are characterized by the N 1 s XPS peaks: 399.50 eV for pyridinic N, 401.06 eV for amino N, 401.79 eV for graphitic N and 405.21 eV for nitro N (Fig. 2 (h)). Table S2 shows that the pyridinic N content of F, N-CDs@bipy increases from 35.74 % to 39.23 % because of the grafting of bipyridine groups. As depicted in Figure S6, the O 1 s XPS spectra reveal three distinct peaks for F, N-CDs and F, N-CDs@bipy at approximately 530.89 eV, 532.58 eV and 535.42 eV, indicative of the chemical environments associated with CO, C-O and C-O-C groups, respectively (Zhang et al., 2021). In addition, the proportion of CO groups in F, N-CDs@bipy increase from 29.46 % to 40.64 % compared to F, N-CDs (Table S3), which corroborates the C 1 s XPS spectral analysis. Notably, the F 1 s XPS spectrum exhibits bimodal signals for semi-ionic C-F and covalent C-F in F, N-CDs (Fig. 2 (i)). In contrast, the deconvoluted F 1 s spectrum of F, N-CDs@bipy exhibits only one bonding configuration at 687.01 eV correlate with semi-ionic C-F, which implies that the covalent C-F bonds are consumed during the nucleophilic substitution reaction (Wang et al., 2022). Table S4 reflects the obvious change of covalent C-F content after grafting, which is consistent with XPS results. It further proves that bipyridine is connected to F, N-CDs through the nucleophilic substitution reaction.

Besides, we also record 2D fluorescence matrix graph maps of F, N-CDs, F, N-CDs@bipy and bipyridine solutions with different pH values for verifying emission origins. As shown in Figure S7, F, N-CDs are slightly quenched in acid and alkali conditions, while bipyridine quenches rapidly under acidic conditions and remains stable under alkaline conditions. The above pH response phenomena of F, N-CDs and bipyridine are consistent with the pH response phenomena of two emission centers of F, N-CDs@bipy. For the detailed analysis, the relative PL intensity of F, N-CDs, F, N-CDs@bipy and bipyridine under different pH values are depicted in Figure S8. Compared to the neutral condition (pH= 7), the PL intensities of Center 1 and F, N-CDs are significantly reduced to 76.24 % and 75.42 % (pH= 2), while to 84.12 % and 85.94 % at pH= 12, respectively. Conversely, the PL intensities of Center 2 and bipyridine are only obviously quenched at pH= 2–21.91 % and 19.75 % and nearly preserved at pH= 12 as 93.45 % and 91.82 %, respectively. The variation in relative PL intensities at different pH levels underscores the distinct origins of dual emission centers: Center 1 is originated from F, N-CDs core structure, while Center 2 is linked to the bipyridine grafting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ and CIP detection by F, N-CDs@bipy
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the practical application of the F, N-CDs@bipy, the optical stability of the sensor including prolonged storage, UV lamp irradiation and ionic strength (based on NaCl concentration) is studied. In Figure S9 (a), the emission intensity ratio exhibits a slight decline within 30 days, indicating exceptional storage stability of the F, N-CDs@bipy. At the same time, the F, N-CDs@bipy reveal excellent fluorescence stability under continuous UV illumination for 30 h (Figure S9 (b)). Furthermore, in high-concentration NaCl solution, the fluorescence intensity ratio of F, N-CDs@bipy presents satisfied stability, indicating the good salt-resistance ability (Figure S9 (c)). All the above results indicate that F, N-CDs@bipy, serving as a nano-sensor, holds significant promise for the analysis of samples in complex environments. Meanwhile, to obtain the optimal sensing capacity, critical detection parameters affecting the experiments are optimized. Firstly, F, N-CDs@bipy concentration (0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10 and 15 mg‧mL−1) and response time (0–15 min) are assessed for Cu2+ and CIP detection. As shown in Figure S10 (a), the fluorescence ratio of 440 nm to 510 nm (F440/F510) decreases gradually within 15 min when 200 μM Cu2+ is added to the F, N-CDs@bipy solutions at concentrations of 0.5, 1 and 2 mg‧mL−1. When the concentration of F, N-CDs@bipy reaches 2 mg‧mL−1, the F440/F510 rapidly decreases from 1.09 to 0.11 in 3 min. This is primarily due to the F/N-related function groups within F, N-CDs@bipy, which facilitates the interaction between the probe and Cu2+, resulting in a swift reaction rate for Cu2+ determination. When the concentration of F, N-CDs@bipy exceeds 5 mg‧mL−1, a progressive rise of the fluorescence ratio is observed and the F440/F510 rises dramatically to 0.57 when the F, N-CDs@bipy concentration reaches 15 mg‧mL−1. It can be explained that the substantial quantity of F, N-CDs@bipy encapsulate each other, thereby diminishing the likelihood of Cu2+ interaction and subsequent reaction. Meanwhile, the recovery trend of F440/F510 of F, N-CDs@bipy-200 μM Cu2+ in 3 min response time satisfies the optimal requirements of detecting CIP (Figure S10 (b)). Generally, 2 mg‧mL−1 of F, N-CDs@bipy and 3 min response time are selected as the optimum conditions of detecting Cu2+ and CIP.

Initially, as shown in Fig. 3 (a), F, N-CDs@bipy yields prominent fluorescence signals at 440 nm and 510 nm (red line). Upon Cu2+ addition, the fluorescence intensity at Center 1 (440 nm) is markedly quenched, while Center 2 (510 nm) yields a significant fluctuation weaker than Center 1 (black line). Remarkably, the bimodal fluorescence intensities are restored when 100 μM of CIP is added to the mixed solution of F, N-CDs@bipy with Cu2+ (blue line). Meanwhile, assessing selectivity is essential for determining the effectiveness of a fluorescent sensor. To evaluate the selectivity of developed nano-sensor for cascade sensing Cu2+ and CIP, thirty-four interfering substances (Ag+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Ce3+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Na+, K+, Res, Cat, HDQ, Pro, Glu, Trp, Leu, L-Cys, L-Ala, L-Ser, L-Asp, PEF, FLE, Lev, TC, OTC, CTC, DOX) are introduced under identical conditions (Fig. 3 (b)). Among all substances, only Cu2+ generates a dramatic decrease in F440/F510. Furthermore, only adding CIP to the mixture of F, N-CDs@bipy and Cu2+ can cause a significant enhancement of F440/F510 in terms of recovery, and there is no apparent enhancement of F440/F510 for other analytes. In Figure S11, interfering substances exert negligible influence on the detection of Cu2+ and CIP. Thus, these results suggest that the F, N-CDs@bipy sensor has a high selectivity toward the detection of Cu2+ and CIP.

The achievement of cascade sensing Cu2+ and CIP sufficiently substantiates the detection capability of F, N-CDs@bipy. As illustrated in Fig. 3 (c), the dual emission peaks of F, N-CDs@bipy have different degrees of quenching response to varying Cu2+ concentration (0–400 μM). In Fig. S12, 2D fluorescence matrix graph maps of F, N-CDs@bipy solution with different Cu2+ concentrations reflect the impact of Cu2+ on the fluorescence intensity of F, N-CDs@bipy intuitively and comprehensively. The F440/F510 falls sharply within the 0–100 μM range and then levels off at higher concentrations (100–400 μM) (Fig. 3 (d)). Fig. 3 (e) shows a strong linear correlation between F440/F510 and Cu2+ concentration (0.056–30 μM). The LOD (3σ/k; σ is the standard deviation of the y-intercepts of regression lines and k is the slope of the calibration graph) and LOQ (10σ/k) are established at 56 nM and 187 nM, which is significantly lower than that derived from single-channel fluorescence measurements at 440 nm (LOD=111 nM and LOQ=370 nM) and 510 nm (LOD=178 nM and LOQ=593 nM) (Figure S13).

Subsequently, F, N-CDs@bipy-Cu2+ exhibits a significant recovery response to the addition of CIP ranging from 0 to 100 μM (Fig. 3 (f)). Corresponding 2D fluorescence matrix graph maps with different concentrations of CIP clearly indicate a more rapid recovery of fluorescence intensity at 440 nm compared to 510 nm (Figure S14). A pronounced linear relationship is constructed across the range of 0.059–20 μM CIP, yielding a R2 value of 0.9975 and the LOD of 59 nM (LOQ=197 nM) (Fig. 3 (g-h)), which is superior to the values obtained through single-wavelength fluorescence intensity assessments at 440 nm (LOD=104 nM; LOQ=347 nM) and 510 nm (LOD=81 nM; LOQ=270 nM) in Figure S15. Furthermore, the fluorescence color of F, N-CDs@bipy exhibits a systematic shift in response to increasing concentrations of Cu2+ and CIP under UV irradiation (Figure S16). Upon the addition of Cu2+, the fluorescence shifts from intense cyan to dim green; however, the addition of CIP brings the dim green gradually back to bright cyan. The pronounced color changes render this ratiometric detection method highly intuitive and precise for sensing Cu2+ and CIP. Furthermore, the developed sensing strategy for Cu2+/CIP in this study shows superior analytical performance when compared to previously reported methods ( Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible underlying mechanism of sensing Cu2+ and CIP
ce:section.ce:para:
Following the introduction of Cu2+ into the F, N-CDs@bipy solution, a drastic decline is noted in the dual-emission fluorescence of F, N-CDs@bipy. Initially, the inner filter (IFE) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) effects can be ruled out and the PET is possible for the F, N-CDs@bipy system, as the absorbance peak of Cu2+ has negligible overlaps with PLE and PL spectra of F, N-CDs@bipy ( Fig. 4 (a)) (Yang et al., 2021a). Nitrogen and fluorine atoms, respectively, contribute five and seven valence electrons, whereas the carbon atom contributes four. As a result, more free electrons appear within the F, N-CDs@bipy after doping nitrogen and fluorine into CDs. Upon adding Cu2+, a strong chelation between Cu2+ and F/N occurs in F, N-CDs@bipy. Based on this, free electrons transfer to the vacant orbitals of Cu2+ under excited state and relax to a lower-energy state without emitting photoluminescence, thereby causing fluorescence quenching in F, N-CDs@bipy (Zhou et al., 2023). In addition, it is well known that the occurrence of PET between the donor and the acceptor enables shortening the fluorescence lifetime, whereas IFE and FRET lack such ability. Utilizing this distinction, we measure the fluorescence lifetime of F, N-CDs@bipy to determine whether there is PET. The fluorescence attenuation curves of dual emission peaks of F, N-CDs@bipy with and without Cu2+ are analyzed under 340 nm excitation (Fig. 4 (b)). The corresponding data is obtained by fitting (Zhou et al., 2023):

representing their respective amplitudes. Corresponding average fluorescence lifetimes of the F, N-CDs@bipy are 4.778 ns (λem= 440 nm) and 4.299 ns (λem= 510 nm). With the addition of Cu2+, the average fluorescence lifetimes of F, N-CDs@bipy decrease rapidly, reaching 2.578 ns (λem=440 nm) and 2.709 ns (λem=510 nm). In addition, according to previous recorded method (Gupta et al., 2011; Jing et al., 2019), the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) are tested to be −5.17 eV and −3.69 eV of F, N-CDs@bipy by cyclic voltammetry, while they are −4.83 eV and −4.63 eV of Cu2+. It illustrates electron absorption of Cu2+ from F, N-CDs@bipy, further verifying that the fluorescence of F, N-CDs@bipy is quenched via PET.

On the other hand, the fluorescence quenching mechanism is elucidated by applying the Stern-Volmer model (Yang et al., 2021b):
(3)

where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of F, N-CDs@bipy with and without Cu2+, respectively; K SV denotes the Stern-Volmer quenching constant; [Q] indicates the concentration of Cu2+; k q is the bimolecular quenching rate constant and τ 0 is the fluorescence lifetime of F, N-CDs@bipy. In Figure S13, K SV is determined to be 0.952 μM−1 (λem= 440 nm) and 1.004 μM−1 (λem= 510 nm) for Cu2+ detection, respectively. Taking τ 0 (4.778 ns, 4.299 ns) and K SV into the formula (2), k q is determined to be 1.99 × 1014 M−1 s−1 and 2.30 × 1013 M−1 s−1, exceeding the typical upper limit (1.0 × 1010) for dynamic quenching processes. The findings confirm that dynamic quenching is improbable within the sensing system, indicating that the observed quenching is attributable to a static quenching mechanism (Yang et al., 2021b). A characteristic feature of static quenching is that the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs can be changed by the quencher because of the formation on a nonfluorescent ground state complex between CDs and the quencher (Wang et al., 2021b). As presented in Fig. 4 (c), the UV–vis absorption peak of F, N-CDs@bipy at 340 nm gradually decreases with the blue shift when adding standard Cu2+ solution. However, the broad absorption band around 410 nm shows the opposite phenomenon, which proves that the difference of surface functional groups between the two fluorescent groups leads to different degree of bimodal quenching (Xu et al., 2021). Therefore, above results confirm that the formation of ground state complexes between F, N-CDs@bipy and Cu2+, coupled with PET principle, affect or destroy the luminescent properties of F, N-CDs@bipy and cause the fluorescence quenching.

The recovery of fluorescence within the system is primarily due to the emulative coordination effect (Han et al., 2021). Studies have shown that the CIP molecule, which possesses a carboxylic acid group, readily forms complexes with metal ions, including Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+, with a particular affinity for Cu2+ (Huang et al., 2022). Cu2+ can strongly bind with abundant carboxylic functional groups of CIP to form stable Cu-CIP conjugate via chelating reaction and inhibit the quenching effiency of Cu2+ to the fluorescence of F, N-CDs@bipy, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence (Cuprys et al., 2018). To support the proposed binding of Cu2+ and CIP, the FT-IR spectra of F, N-CDs@bipy, F, N-CDs@bipy-Cu2+, F, N-CDs@bipy-Cu2+-CIP are recorded (Fig. 4 (d)). Compared with the F, N-CDs@bipy, the stretching vibrations of -COOH (1400 cm−1) and O-H/N-H (3456 cm−1) in F, N-CDs@bipy-Cu2+ are significantly decreased, and a pronounced absorption peak associated with the C-N-Cu bond is developed at 1521 cm−1, indicating the specific interactions of Cu2+ to functional groups of F, N-CDs@bipy (non-fluorescent complexes). Afterward, with the addition of CIP, the significantly increased absorption of -COOH and O-H/N-H bonds are recorded, indicating that Cu2+ is removed from the F, N-CDs@bipy surface by chelating reaction with CIP. More importantly, the appearance of a characteristic absorption peak at 2000 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum of F, N-CDs@bipy-Cu2+-CIP confirms the formation of Cu-CIP chelate (Huang et al., 2022). Consequently, the chelating reaction between CIP and Cu2+ leads to the fluorescence recovery. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of F, N-CDs@bipy also returns its original value (Figure S17), which proves the detachment of Cu2+ from the F, N-CDs@bipy surface. This aligns with the recovery of absorption peaks in the UV–vis spectrum ( Figure S18 ). From these results, the schematic illustration of underlying mechanism for cascade sensing Cu2+ and CIP is depicted in Fig. 4 (e).

In general, the human eye cannot discern color changes associated with fluorescence intensity variations of less than 15 % (Yang et al., 2021b). To facilitate the convenient and instant determination of Cu2+ and CIP, we design a handheld fluorescence sensing platform integrated a smartphone featuring the Red-Green-Blue (RGB) recognizer application (app) and a portable detection box. The exterior of the box is coated with black paint to minimize the infiltration of external light, which ensures that the final collected photo color comes from the fluorescence of the analytical sample. The integrated equipment design and the detection process is presented in Video S1. Fig. 5 (a-b) show the hardware configuration of the smartphone fluorometric sensing platform. The device comprises two parts: the bottom part loaded with a tube and the top part housing the excitation light source. This box is equipped with micro-UV lamp, push-pull device, optical filter, sample holder, air chamber, mirror and viewfinder window (Fig. 5 (c)). The micro-UV lamp served as the excitation light is connected to a Li-ion 18650 battery for power and its light is directed through a 340 nm filter before illuminating the sample. The fluorescence emission of the sample is reflected via a mirror into the smartphone camera, which is outfitted with a 20-megapixel sensor. More importantly, this handheld platform integrated high-performance fluorescence nanomaterials and smartphone can be unaffected by environmental factors and holds promise for accurate sensing from the scene. Under UV illumination, the distinct fluorescence images of F, N-CDs@bipy solution, corresponding to varying Cu2+ and CIP concentrations, are recorded by a smartphone camera, in which the color changes from bright cyan to dim green to bright cyan (Fig. 5 (d)). The RGB values corresponding to these photographs are extracted using the "Color Captor" app, facilitating subsequent analysis and statistical processing (Fig. 5 (e)). Fig. 5 (f-g) illustrate the correlation between the G/B ratio and concentrations of Cu2+ and CIP, with detection limits are 112 nM (linear range 0.112–30 μM, Cu2+) and 126 μM (linear range 0.126–20 μM, CIP). Subsequently, repeated measurements on the F, N-CDs@bipy fluorescence sensors responses to individual Cu2+ (2 μM) and CIP (1 μM) are conducted, yielding the relative standard deviation (%RSD) of 0.38 % and 0.45 % (repeating 10 times), which demonstrates the good uniformity and replicability of the assay (Fig. 5 (h)). In addition, Figure S19 shows that the smartphone fluorometric sensing platform is highly selective for Cu2+ and CIP analysis. Besides, we also measure Cu2+ and CIP in tap water and meat samples to validate the practicability of the F, N-CDs@bipy. The recoveries ranging from 94.0 % to 105.0 % and the %RSD of less than 2.59 % affirm the high accuracy and reliability for practical applications, as detailed in Table 2.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.107172.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1

In summary, an effective strategy for synthesizing F, N-CDs@bipy via grafting bipyridine on F, N-CDs has been successfully developed, featuring dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence for cascade sensing Cu2+ and CIP. The blue emission at 440 nm originates from the F, N-CDs and the green emission (510 nm) is attributed to the grafted bipyridine functional group. Based on the PET and static complex formation, the F, N-CDs@bipy-based sensor demonstrates exceptional sensitivity for Cu2+ detection with a broad dynamic range from 0.056 to 30 μM and a LOD as low as 56 nM. Furthermore, following the introduction of CIP, fluorescence is notably restored attributed to the Cu-CIP complexation and the subsequent liberation of F, N-CDs@bipy. The engineered F, N-CDs@bipy-Cu2+ nano-sensor exhibits effective CIP detection over a concentration range of 0.059–20 μM, exhibiting a biphasic linear response a LOD at 59 nM. Besides, a smartphone fluorometric sensing platform is designed to achieve the expeditious, accurate and on-site detection of Cu2+ and CIP in real samples by analyzing RGB values. This study's findings highlight the potential and advantages of the F, N-CDs@bipy-based nano-sensor for cascade detection of Cu2+ and CIP, showcasing its suitability for future real-time monitoring applications in food safety and environmental pollution assessment.
